APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The paper addresses automatic evaluation of end-to-end data-driven NLG, a core NLP topic directly relevant to top NLP/ML venues. It targets the right audience (NLG and evaluation researchers) and formulates a clear, well-scoped problem (reliability of 21 automatic metrics across three systems and three datasets). The methodology and evaluation (multiple systems/datasets, human Likert ratings, broad set of metrics, and a proposed composite metric RAINBOW with code released) meet the standards expected at top conferences. Positioning and related work coverage are appropriate, with limitations and dataset/system-specific findings clearly stated.

IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles an important problem—reliability of automatic metrics for end-to-end data-driven NLG—and provides a comprehensive empirical study (21 metrics, three systems RNNLG/TGEN/LOLS, three datasets SFHOTEL/SFREST/BAGEL) showing weak sentence-level correlation with human judgements. Its finding that metric performance is system- and data-specific and that metrics poorly separate medium/good outputs is likely to change how researchers evaluate NLG systems. The proposed composite metric RAINBOW, achieving much higher sentence-level correlation (up to ρ=0.81) and more stable results, is likely to be adopted and stimulate follow-up work on composite and grammar-aware metrics. While highly impactful for the NLG evaluation community, the contribution is primarily methodological within the subfield rather than broadly transformative across NLP, so I rate it strong impact (4).

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by experiments comparing three systems across three datasets using 21 metrics and crowd-sourced human judgements, and the proposed composite (RAINBOW) is reported with strong sentence-level correlation (ρ up to 0.81). There are no mathematical derivations to verify, and the experimental setup uses appropriate baselines and a wide set of metrics; however, the summary omits details on statistical testing and hyperparameter reporting (reproducibility concern) and notes grammar-based measures are preliminary and the parser score may be an imperfect proxy for grammaticality. Overall the methodology is sound but limited by dataset/system scope and some missing experimental details, so minor issues do not undermine the main conclusions.

CLARITY.justification: The paper appears well-organized with a clear experimental flow from methodology to experiments and conclusion, and its main contributions (large-scale metric comparison and the RAINBOW composite metric) are stated early. The writing quality is described as good and related work coverage adequate, suggesting notation and definitions are mostly consistent and understandable. A comprehensive set of metrics and datasets are presented, and figures/tables/examples seem to support the evaluation, though a few presentation details (e.g., reporting of hyperparameters) are missing.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper makes a notable, though not revolutionary, contribution: combining a broad set of 21 automated metrics (including novel grammar-based measures) and evaluating them across three systems and three datasets is a clear extension beyond earlier metric-comparison studies (e.g., prior NLG/MT metric evaluations and calls for new metrics [1–4]). The RAINBOW composite metric and the claim of being first to apply grammar-based measures (GBMs) for sentence-level NLG evaluation provide a distinct methodological contribution. However, the core problem (evaluating correlation between automatic metrics and human judgments) is well-studied (see survey/validation works from 2006–2017), so the novelty is substantial but incremental rather than groundbreaking.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The experiments are broad and well-designed: three diverse end-to-end NLG systems, three datasets, 21 automatic metrics (including new grammar-based measures), and crowdsourced human judgments support the main claims. The paper reports and discusses limitations (sentence-level unreliability, dataset- and system-specificity, and preliminary nature of grammar-based measures), but the summary does not indicate ablation or sensitivity analyses for the proposed RAINBOW composite metric, which is an important omission. Related work coverage is described as adequate and aligns with prior evaluations of metric validity (e.g., studies calling for new NLG metrics and investigations into MT/NLG metric correlation), and reproducibility is partially supported by released code though hyperparameters are not reported. Overall this is a solid and comprehensive study with some gaps in analysis and reproducibility details.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper evaluates three standard end-to-end data-driven NLG systems (RNNLG, TGEN, LOLS) across three datasets and 21 automatic metrics, and uses human judgments collected under the same conditions—this is a comprehensive and fair experimental comparison. It also analyzes metric behavior across systems and datasets, acknowledges metric limitations, and proposes a composite (RAINBOW) shown to outperform individual metrics; related work coverage is reported as adequate and includes the key prior studies on metric validity (e.g., METEOR and survey/validation papers). Given this thorough, dataset- and system-consistent evaluation and explicit discussion of prior findings, the comparisons are comprehensive and appropriate.

main_claims: Automatic metrics (word-overlap and grammar-based) only weakly reflect human judgements for end-to-end data-driven NLG and are particularly unreliable at the sentence level.
This work is the first to evaluate a large set (21) of automated metrics including grammar-based measures on end-to-end data-driven NLG across three systems and three datasets.
They propose a new combined metric, RAINBOW, which achieves much higher sentence-level correlation with human judgements (up to ρ=0.81) and shows more stable results across systems and datasets.
Automatic metric performance is data- and system-specific and metrics distinguish low-quality outputs better than average or good outputs.
_summary.methodology: Compare three end-to-end data-driven NLG systems (RNNLG, TGEN, LOLS) on three crowdsourced datasets (SFHOTEL, SFREST, BAGEL), evaluate outputs with 21 automatic metrics (including novel grammar-based metrics), collect human Likert-scale ratings (informativeness, naturalness, quality) from crowdworkers, analyse correlations and errors, and propose the RAINBOW composite metric.
limitations: Automatic metrics are not reliable at the sentence level.
Metric performance is dataset- and system-specific.
Metrics perform poorly at distinguishing outputs of medium and good quality.
Grammar-based measures are preliminary; the Stanford parser score is not designed specifically to measure grammaticality.
